task_summary: Fix clip resize behavior so video clips adjust speed (not trim) when resized, and audio clips are clamped to source duration. Currently, `useClipResize.ts` recalculates `from`/`to` on resize without touching `speed`, causing freeze-frame or cutoff. The fix requires track-kind-aware logic: video clips preserve from/to and compute new speed, audio clips keep current behavior but clamp to asset duration.

key_evidence.point: useClipResize.onActionResizeEnd currently recalculates `from` (left edge) and `to` (right edge) using getSourceTime, but never modifies `speed`
The `dir` parameter (left/right) is already passed through to onActionResizeEnd but the hook signature destructures it as `{ action, row, dir: string }` — it's available but unused
Track kind is accessible via `dataRef.current.tracks` which contains TrackDefinition[] with `kind: 'visual' | 'audio'`. ClipMeta has `track` field mapping to track ID
Asset duration is available via `dataRef.current.resolvedConfig.clips` where each resolved clip has `assetEntry?.duration`. The registry is also on `dataRef.current.registry`
getSourceTime formula: `clip.from + (time - clip.start) * clip.speed` — used in useClipResize, rowsToConfig, and multi-drag-utils
TimelineCanvas.updateResize handles live drag preview — audio clamping needs to happen here to prevent visual extension past limit. ResizeSession stores actionId/rowId but not track kind
resolveOverlaps also adjusts from/to based on speed when trimming overlapping clips — overlap resolution will need to be speed-aware for video clips too
rowsToConfig serialization uses getSourceTime to compute `to` from action.end — with speed-based resize this still works correctly since speed is stored in clipMeta
VisualClip and AudioTrack both read `clip.speed` as `playbackRate` — speed changes in useClipResize will automatically propagate to rendering
The onActionResizeEnd callback is passed dir but the UseClipResizeResult interface types it as `dir: string` not `ResizeDir` — already wired through

relevant_code.why: PRIMARY: Main resize logic. Must add track-kind branching: video clips update speed instead of from/to, audio clips clamp to asset duration

Live drag preview needs audio clamping in updateResize() to prevent dragging past source duration. Also needs visual feedback when hitting audio limit. ResizeSession may need extra metadata (track kind, asset duration)
getSourceTime formula is central to resize math. rowsToConfig serializes from/to/speed — verify speed changes serialize correctly. TimelineData interface provides access to tracks and resolvedConfig
getClipTimelineDuration and getClipSourceDuration define the duration math. Speed-based resize must be consistent with these formulas
Overlap resolution trims clips and adjusts from/to with speed. For video clips that use speed-based resize, overlap trimming may need different handling (trim via speed change, not from/to change)
Uses getSourceTime to compute `to` after drag — same pattern as resize. May need similar speed-aware logic if multi-drag also extends clips
Defines TrackKind, TrackDefinition, AssetRegistryEntry (with duration field), ResolvedTimelineClip
Wires clipResize callbacks through context — if new props are needed (e.g. passing tracks info for clamping), this is the plumbing layer

test_expectations.what_it_checks: Resize pending ops tracking (increment/decrement of pendingOpsRef, cancelled resize). Does NOT test from/to/speed math — should still pass.

constraints: Hold-type clips (images/text) must be excluded from speed logic — they already have a separate code path (typeof clipMeta.hold === 'number')
resolveOverlaps patches from/to based on speed — if video clips now use speed-based resize, overlap resolution for video clips must not corrupt the speed/from/to relationship
rowsToConfig serialization uses getSourceTime(from, start, speed, end) to compute `to` — speed must be written to clipMeta before serialization
Audio clips must never have speed changed by resize (audio pitch would change)
VisualClip and AudioTrack read playbackRate from clip.speed — any speed value set during resize will affect playback immediately
Multi-drag (planMultiDragMoves in multi-drag-utils.ts) uses similar getSourceTime pattern — evaluate if it needs the same speed treatment

getClipTimelineDuration is used across migrate.ts, editor-utils.ts, config-utils.ts — it divides source duration by speed, so speed changes will automatically reflect in timeline duration calculations
suggested_approach:
**Phase 1: useClipResize.ts — Track-kind-aware resize logic**
- In `onActionResizeStart`, also save `originalTo`, `originalSpeed`, and the track kind (look up via `dataRef.current.tracks.find(t => t.id === clipMeta.track)?.kind`). For audio clips, also save `assetDuration` from `dataRef.current.resolvedConfig.clips.find(c => c.id === action.id)?.assetEntry?.duration`.
- In `onActionResizeEnd`, branch on track kind:
  - **Visual clips (right-edge)**: Keep `from` fixed, compute `newSpeed = (originalTo - originalFrom) / (action.end - action.start)`. Set `speed` in metaUpdates, do NOT change `to`.
  - **Visual clips (left-edge)**: Adjust `from` as now, then recalculate `speed = (to - newFrom) / (action.end - action.start)`.
  - **Audio clips**: Keep current from/to logic but clamp `to` to `assetDuration` and clamp timeline end accordingly.

**Phase 2: TimelineCanvas.tsx — Audio clamping during drag**
- In `updateResize`, for audio clips, clamp `end` so the clip cannot extend beyond `(assetDuration - from) / speed + start`. This requires passing track kind and asset info to the canvas (via an `onGetResizeConstraints` callback or by enriching ResizeSession with max-end at session start time).
- Add a visual cue (e.g. a CSS class on the clip or a subtle indicator) when the audio clip hits its max extent.

**Phase 3: resolveOverlaps consideration**
- For video clips, when overlap resolution trims the clip, it should adjust speed instead of from/to. This may require passing track kind into resolveOverlaps, or the caller (useClipResize) should post-process the overlap patches to convert from/to adjustments into speed adjustments for visual tracks.
